outline.heading: Abstract
outline.preview:
# Abstract Background Tyrosol is an important drug precursor, and Saccharomyces cerevisiae is one of the main microorganisms that produces tyrosol. Although excessive metabolic modification increases the production of tyrosol, it also causes a decrease in the growth rate of yeast. Therefore, this study attempted to restore the growth of S. cerevisiae through adaptive evolution and further improve tyrosol production.

reading_plan.reading_goal: Reconstruct the research evolution trajectory: starting from the problem of growth impairment in a high-tyrosol-producing S. cerevisiae strain (S26), through adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) to restore growth and further improve tyrosol yield, identification of key mutations (SNZ3 Val125Ile and Pho3 Asn134Asp) via transcriptome and SNP analysis, computational docking to hypothesize their effects, and reverse engineering to validate their roles in enhancing growth and robustness.

must_find: Initial problem: growth impairment in strain S26 due to prior metabolic engineering for tyrosol overproduction.
ALE design: stepwise increase in glucose concentration, duration, and selection criteria.
Phenotypic changes: growth rate, biomass, tyrosol titer, and byproducts in evolved strains vs. parent.
Transcriptomic differences: upregulated/downregulated pathways (glycolysis, TCA cycle, tyrosol synthesis) in S26-AE2.
SNP mutations: specific genes (SNZ3, PHO3) and amino acid changes, and their predicted effects.
Molecular docking results: binding affinity changes for substrates (thiamine phosphate, D-ribose 5-phosphate).
Reverse engineering: construction of single and double mutants, their growth and tyrosol production compared to parent and evolved strain.
Validation of mechanism: how SNZ3 and PHO3 mutations contribute to improved growth and robustness.
Limitations: any trade-offs, incomplete restoration, or other constraints mentioned.
risk_notes: The paper is long and dense; key details may be spread across multiple sections, requiring careful cross-referencing.
Transcriptome and SNP analyses are correlative; causal links rely on reverse engineering, so focus on the logical flow from observation to validation.
Some methods (e.g., molecular docking) are computational predictions; note the distinction between in silico and in vivo evidence.
The combined markdown may have formatting artifacts; verify critical data (e.g., figures, tables) in the original PDF if needed.

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The initial problem was that the high-tyrosol-producing S. cerevisiae strain S26, constructed via extensive metabolic engineering, suffered from impaired growth, limiting industrial applicability. The goal was to restore growth and further improve tyrosol yield using adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE), then identify the genetic and transcriptomic mechanisms behind the improved phenotype, and validate key mutations via reverse engineering.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: ALE of S. cerevisiae S26 yielded three evolved strains. S26-AE2 showed 15.33% higher biomass (17.82 g DCW/L) and 817.83 mg/L tyrosol in 100 g/L glucose. Transcriptome analysis revealed reduced HXK2 expression (less glucose repression), increased pyruvate synthesis genes, and higher TCA cycle gene expression in 20 g/L glucose. SNP analysis identified SNZ3 Val125Ile and PHO3 Asn134Asp mutations. Reverse engineering confirmed SNZ3 mutation increased tyrosol by 17.01% in 100 g/L glucose.
process_signals: ALE successfully restored growth and increased tyrosol production.
Transcriptome and SNP analyses provided mechanistic hypotheses.
Reverse engineering validated the role of SNZ3 mutation.

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: Tyrosol is a valuable drug precursor. S. cerevisiae S26 was previously engineered for high tyrosol yield but excessive modification reduced robustness and growth, limiting industrial use. ALE is a proven method to improve growth defects and understand molecular evolution. This study aimed to use ALE to restore growth and increase tyrosol, then apply transcriptomics, SNP analysis, and reverse engineering to elucidate mechanisms.
process_signals: Initial strain S26 had growth impairment due to metabolic engineering.
ALE was chosen to restore growth and potentially improve tyrosol production.
Plan to combine ALE with omics and reverse engineering for mechanistic insight.
failures_or_limits: Excessive metabolic modification decreased yeast robustness.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: This is a section heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Adaptive laboratory evolution of S. cerevisiae S26 for improved growth phenotypes
section_gists.gist: ALE was performed in YEPD with stepwise glucose increases: Stage 1 (20→40 g/L, 16 h avg passage) yielded S26-AE1; Stage 2 (40→80 g/L, 11.75 h avg passage) yielded S26-AE2; Stage 3 (80→125 g/L, 11.95 h avg passage) yielded S26-AE3. SEM showed some cell damage in S26-AE2 but not worsened in S26-AE3. Spot assays showed all evolved strains grew better than S26 on 20 and 100 g/L glucose.
process_signals: ALE successfully improved growth on high glucose.
Passage time decreased from stage 1 to 2, indicating adaptation.
S26-AE2 showed cell damage but still better growth.
methods: Adaptive laboratory evolution in EVOL cells with automatic passaging at OD600=15
failures_or_limits: S26-AE2 showed obvious cell damage under SEM, possibly due to premature stationary phase or high sugar.

section_gists.heading: Changes in the fermentation performance of evolutionary strains
section_gists.gist: In 100 g/L glucose YEPD, S26 entered stationary phase at 48 h (OD600 36.39, 15.45 g DCW/L). S26-AE1 biomass was 8.99% higher. S26-AE2 entered stationary phase earlier (24 h), OD600 38.45, biomass 17.82 g DCW/L (15.33% higher). S26-AE3 also early stationary phase, OD600 39.14. Tyrosol: S26 max 483.35 mg/L at 60 h; S26-AE1 not significantly different; S26-AE2 reached 817.83 mg/L at 48 h (69.20% higher than S26-AE1); S26-AE3 553.79 mg/L at 72 h. Glucose consumption: S26 residual 89.30 g/L at 12 h; S26-AE1 38.05 g/L; S26-AE2 and S26-AE3 completely consumed glucose by 12 h. Ethanol: S26 4.20 g/L, S26-AE1 23.08 g/L, S26-AE2 34.16 g/L, S26-AE3 36.12 g/L at 12 h. Trehalose: S26 and S26-AE1 accumulated extracellular trehalose; S26-AE2 and S26-AE3 produced less.
process_signals: S26-AE2 showed best combination of growth and tyrosol production.
Evolved strains consumed glucose faster and produced more ethanol, indicating metabolic shift.
S26-AE2 selected for further analysis due to superior performance.
methods: Fermentation in YEPD with 100 g/L glucose
OD600 and dry cell weight measurement
failures_or_limits: S26-AE3 had lower tyrosol than S26-AE2 despite good growth.
S26-AE1 did not improve tyrosol significantly.

section_gists.heading: Transcriptome analysis of the mechanisms underlying adaptive evolution leading to increased tyrosol production in strain S26-AE2
section_gists.gist: Transcriptome compared S26 and S26-AE2 in 20 and 100 g/L glucose. In 100 vs 20 g/L, S26 had 807 up, 583 down; S26-AE2 had 779 up, 621 down. S26-AE2 vs S26 in 20 g/L: 36 up, 1 down; in 100 g/L: 9 up, 16 down. Key findings: HXT5 (low/medium-affinity glucose transporter) upregulated in S26-AE2 in 20 g/L. HXK2 expression lower in S26-AE2 under 100 g/L, suggesting reduced glucose repression. Pyruvate synthesis genes (PYK2, PDC1, PDC5, PDC6) upregulated in S26-AE2. TCA cycle genes (CIT1, ACO1, IDH1, IDH2, KGD1, KGD2, LSC1, LSC2, SDH1, SDH2, SDH3, SDH4, FUM1, MDH1) mostly upregulated in S26-AE2 in 20 g/L. Tyrosol synthesis genes (ARO10, ARO8, ARO9) upregulated in S26-AE2.
process_signals: Transcriptome provided hypotheses for improved growth and tyrosol: reduced glucose repression, increased pyruvate and TCA cycle, upregulated tyrosol pathway.
Differences in gene expression between strains and conditions guided focus on specific pathways.
failures_or_limits: Transcriptome is correlative; causal links need validation.

section_gists.heading: SNP mutation analysis of the adaptive laboratory evolutionary strain S26-AE2
section_gists.gist: Genome sequencing of S26-AE2 identified 19 SNP mutations across comparisons. Five genes had SNPs: MAL32, SNZ3, DOT6, HXT3, PHO3. SNZ3 is a pyridoxal 5'-phosphate synthase subunit, induced in stationary phase and thiamine absence. PHO3 is a constitutively expressed acid phosphatase. These mutations were hypothesized to affect thiamine/pyridoxine metabolism and thus tyrosol production.
process_signals: SNP analysis narrowed down candidate mutations responsible for evolved phenotype.
SNZ3 and PHO3 selected for further study based on known functions.
failures_or_limits: Only 19 SNPs found; other mutations may exist outside coding regions.

section_gists.heading: Molecular docking analysis of the SNZ3 and PHO3 mutants binding to small molecules
section_gists.gist: Docking simulations: PHO3 Asn134Asp mutation decreased Vina score for thiamine phosphate to -6.6 (improved affinity) and for thiamine to -5.4. Residue interactions changed. SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation decreased Vina score for D-ribose 5-phosphate to -5.7, but no residue change; for pyridoxal, score unchanged (-5.6) but THR4 and ALA69 binding increased. Results suggest mutations may enhance substrate binding.
process_signals: Molecular docking provided computational support for functional impact of mutations.
Improved binding affinity suggests mutations could increase enzyme activity.
failures_or_limits: Docking is in silico prediction; experimental validation needed.
SNZ3 mutation did not change binding residues, only score.

section_gists.heading: Reverse engineering verification improves yeast growth and robustness
section_gists.gist: CRISPR-Cas9 used to introduce SNZ3 Val125Ile and PHO3 Asn134Asp mutations into S26, creating single and double mutants. In 20 g/L glucose, S26-SNZ3 Mut increased tyrosol by 5.61% at 60 h. In 100 g/L glucose, S26-SNZ3 Mut increased tyrosol by 17.01% at 72 h. Double mutant increased tyrosol by 11.37% at 72 h. PHO3 single mutant effect not explicitly stated. Results confirm SNZ3 mutation promotes tyrosol production, especially in high glucose.
process_signals: Reverse engineering validated SNZ3 mutation's positive effect on tyrosol.
Double mutant did not outperform single SNZ3 mutant, suggesting possible interaction or saturation.
methods: Fermentation in YEPD with 20 or 100 g/L glucose
failures_or_limits: PHO3 single mutant effect not clearly reported.
Double mutant had lower tyrosol increase than SNZ3 single mutant (11.37% vs 17.01%).

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: ALE restored growth and improved tyrosol in S26-AE2. Transcriptome revealed changes in glycolysis, TCA cycle, and tyrosol synthesis. Genome analysis identified PHO3 and SNZ3 mutations. Molecular docking suggested enhanced substrate binding. Reverse engineering confirmed SNZ3 mutation increases tyrosol. Hypothesis: mutations increase pyridoxine and thiamine synthesis, boosting phosphoketolase activity and E4P precursor for tyrosol. Not all mutations beneficial; PHO6 mutant auxotrophic for thiamine.
process_signals: Integrated omics and reverse engineering to propose mechanism.
Acknowledged that not all mutations are beneficial.
Proposed thiamine/pyridoxine link to tyrosol via phosphoketolase.
failures_or_limits: Mechanism is hypothetical; direct enzyme activity not measured.
Other mutations (MAL32, DOT6, HXT3) not explored.

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: ALE restored growth of S26. Transcriptome showed expression differences in glycolysis, TCA cycle, tyrosol synthesis. Molecular docking and reverse engineering validated SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation increases tyrosol. Study provides basis for metabolic engineering of tyrosol production.
process_signals: Successfully achieved goal of restoring growth and improving tyrosol.
Validated key mutation.

section_gists.heading: Materials and methods
section_gists.gist: This is a section heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Adaptive laboratory evolution
section_gists.gist: S26 was evolved in EVOL cells with automatic passaging when OD600 reached 15. Initial glucose 20 g/L, gradually increased. Samples collected at each stage, preserved in glycerol. Average passage time calculated per stage.
process_signals: ALE protocol: automatic passaging at fixed OD600, stepwise glucose increase.
Selection based on growth under increasing glucose stress.
methods: EVOL cell system for automatic passaging

section_gists.heading: Analytical methods
section_gists.gist: OD600 measured by spectrophotometer. Tyrosol and trehalose by HPLC with C18 column, gradient elution, UV detection at 224 nm for tyrosol, refractive index for trehalose. Glucose and ethanol by biosensor. Glycerol by TG assay kit. Cell morphology by SEM.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: ALE under increasing glucose selected for mutations that restore growth and enhance tyrosol production by altering central carbon metabolism and thiamine/pyridoxine synthesis.
SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation increases pyridoxal phosphate synthesis, which may boost thiamine production, activating phosphoketolase and increasing E4P precursor for tyrosol.
PHO3 Asn134Asp mutation may improve thiamine phosphate hydrolysis, contributing to thiamine availability, but its effect on tyrosol is less clear than SNZ3.
Transcriptomic changes (reduced HXK2, increased TCA cycle) support a shift toward respiratory metabolism and reduced glucose repression, improving growth efficiency.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What is the exact effect of PHO3 Asn134Asp single mutation on growth and tyrosol? The text does not clearly state its individual impact.
Why did the double mutant (SNZ3+PHO3) show lower tyrosol increase than SNZ3 single mutant? Possible epistasis or metabolic burden.
Are the transcriptomic changes directly caused by the identified SNPs, or are they secondary adaptations?

The goal was to restore growth and further improve tyrosol yield via adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE), then identify and validate the underlying genetic and transcriptomic mechanisms.

canonical_events.summary: S. cerevisiae S26, engineered for high tyrosol, suffers from impaired growth, limiting industrial use.
ALE can restore growth and further improve tyrosol production in S26.
ALE protocol: automatic passaging at OD600=15 with stepwise glucose increase (20→40→80→125 g/L).
ALE performed in three stages, yielding evolved strains S26-AE1, S26-AE2, S26-AE3 with decreasing passage times.
Fermentation in 100 g/L glucose: S26-AE2 achieved 15.33% higher biomass (17.82 g DCW/L) and 817.83 mg/L tyrosol (69.20% higher than S26-AE1).
Evolved strains S26-AE2 and S26-AE3 completely consumed glucose by 12 h and produced more ethanol, indicating faster glucose utilization.
Transcriptome analysis: S26-AE2 vs S26 in 20 g/L glucose showed 36 upregulated, 1 downregulated gene; in 100 g/L, 9 upregulated, 16 downregulated.
Reduced HXK2 expression in S26-AE2 under 100 g/L glucose suggests decreased glucose repression, contributing to improved growth.
Upregulation of pyruvate synthesis genes (PGK1, GPM1, GPM2, ENO1/ENO2, PYK1) and TCA cycle genes in S26-AE2, indicating enhanced central metabolism.
SNP analysis of S26-AE2 identified 19 SNPs, including nonsynonymous mutations in SNZ3 (Val125Ile) and PHO3 (Asn134Asp).
Molecular docking: SNZ3 Val125Ile improved binding to D-ribose 5-phosphate (Vina score -5.7); PHO3 Asn134Asp improved binding to thiamine phosphate (Vina score -6.6).
Reverse engineering: SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation increased tyrosol by 17.01% in 100 g/L glucose, confirming its role.
Double mutant (SNZ3+PHO3) increased tyrosol by only 11.37%, less than SNZ3 single mutant, suggesting possible interaction or saturation.
PHO3 single mutant effect not clearly reported; other SNP genes (MAL32, DOT6, HXT3) not explored; mechanism remains hypothetical without direct enzyme activity measurements.

initial_request.research_problem: Excessive metabolic engineering in S. cerevisiae S26 increased tyrosol but impaired growth, limiting industrial applicability. The goal was to restore growth and further improve tyrosol yield via adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE), then identify and validate the underlying genetic and transcriptomic mechanisms.

constraints: Industrial applicability requires both high tyrosol yield and robust growth.
quantifiable_goals: Increase tyrosol yield beyond current levels
